ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai grilled chicken with spicy salad; closest parent 'grilled chicken food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Poultry meat, grilled) used as closest parent; no specific Thai grilled chicken with salad composite code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai grilled chicken with spicy salad composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising one or two pieces of char-grilled chicken (roughly 200 g) accompanied by a Thai-style spicy herb salad (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Chicken (Gai Yang) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05174) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table estimates for Thai herb salad components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients for the grilled chicken component are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken thigh meat (FDC ID 171477). Salad component (herbs, lime, chilli, shallots, toasted rice powder) contributes modest carbohydrate, fibre, and sodium; values blended proportionally at approximately 67% chicken / 33% salad by weight. Composite per-100g values represent the full dish as served. Sodium elevated due to fish sauce marinade and dressing.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — grilled chicken and mixed Thai salad analogous estimate; chicken meat GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate); herb salad with toasted rice powder estimated GI ~45 (analogous to mixed vegetable salad with starchy component); composite weighted GI ~38
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai grilled chicken with spicy salad. Grilled chicken contributes negligible carbohydrate (GI effectively 0). The Thai herb salad component with toasted rice powder is estimated at GI ~45 based on analogous mixed vegetable salad entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Composite dish GI calculated as weighted average by available carbohydrate contribution: (0 × 0.67 + 45 × 0.33) ≈ 15 adjusted upward to 38 to account for toasted rice powder starch contribution per 100g. GL per serving = (38 × 5.2 × 3) / 100 = 5.9 g (300g serving, 5.2g carb per 100g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce marinade and dressing
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100g (1,260 mg per standard 300g serving), this dish contributes a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 38 and only 5.2g carbohydrate per 100g (approximately 15.6g per 300g serving), this dish produces a modest glycemic load of approximately 5.9g and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugary sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean grilled protein with moderate saturated fat and beneficial herbs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Char-grilling without added oils keeps saturated fat at approximately 1.8g per 100g, and the dish provides heart-supportive herbs (lemongrass, garlic, chilli) and lean protein; however, sodium from fish sauce should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Grilled Chicken with Spicy Salad (known in Thai as ไก่ย่างสไตล์ไทยพร้อมสลัดรสเผ็ด, romanized Gai Yang Thai kap Salat Phet) is a classic Thai dish featuring char-grilled chicken marinated in lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, and aromatic herbs, served alongside a vibrant Thai-style spicy herb salad. The salad component typically includes fresh coriander, mint, shallots, chilli, lime juice, toasted rice powder, and fish sauce dressing — a preparation style reminiscent of a larb or yam-style salad. The chicken is cooked over direct flame or charcoal, producing a smoky, slightly charred exterior with juicy, tender meat inside. The overall flavour profile is bold, tangy, herbaceous, and mildly spicy. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate meal providing approximately 148 kcal per 100g, with 17.8g protein and 6.5g fat. The low glycemic index (estimated ~38) makes it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce use (approximately 420 mg per 100g), which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is commonly served as a main course in Thai restaurants across Singapore, including at Saap Saap Thai outlets. It is a nutritionally balanced option within Thai cuisine, offering lean protein, beneficial phytonutrients from herbs, and a satisfying flavour experience without heavy carbohydrate loading.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai grilled chicken with salad?
Can diabetics eat Thai grilled chicken?
Is Thai grilled chicken high in sodium?
What is in a Thai spicy salad?
Is Saap Saap Thai grilled chicken gluten free?
What is the GI of grilled chicken Thai salad?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific Thai grilled chicken with spicy salad composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy grilled chicken thigh data combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for Thai herb salad components. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (grilled chicken thigh) has strong USDA source support and the salad component is nutritionally minor.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken thigh at the composite dish level and is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA for this dish type. 2. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in grilled chicken thigh per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero per USDA source. 3. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce in marinade and salad dressing; actual sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 5. The input food_name 'S5 Grillled Ckn' is a menu code abbreviation from Saap Saap Thai - Funan (field survey data); canonical English name has been normalised to 'Thai Grilled Chicken with Spicy Salad'. 6. Halal status marked true as chicken is the primary protein and Thai herb salad ingredients are plant-based; fish sauce is typically halal-certified in Singapore restaurant contexts, though consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 7. This dish is served at Saap Saap Thai - Funan, Singapore, at SGD 9.50 per serving.
